title: 1965: A Computer You Could Actually Buy
lesson:
Seventeen years after the Baby, the time machine stops in 1965. In front of you is a DEC PDP-8, about the size of a fridge, with a front panel of switches and blinking lamps. This is the machine that changed who got to use computers. An IBM mainframe cost hundreds of thousands of dollars and lived in a guarded room with its own staff. The PDP-8 cost about $18,500, ran on ordinary power, and could sit on a lab bench. DEC sold around 50,000 of them, the first minicomputer that was a real hit. For the first time, a single research group, factory, or hospital could own a computer outright.

Architecturally it's a big step up from the Baby. Words are 12 bits, memory is 4096 words, and there's a one-bit Link (L) that catches carries. And, at last, it can really add: TAD ('Two's-complement ADd') adds a memory word to the accumulator. (The PDP-8 world counted in octal. We'll use decimal here for clarity, but it's worth knowing the real programmers thought in base 8.)

Notice the new convenience in addressing: instructions name a memory spot by label, and the assembler works out how to reach it. You're no longer placing every word by hand.
prompt: Work out 5 plus 7 and leave 12 in the accumulator (AC). Start by clearing AC with CLA, then use TAD to add each value. Finish with HLT.
starterCode:
*200            / the program loads at location 200
        CLA     / AC = 0
        TAD A   / AC = AC + 5
        TAD B   / AC = AC + 7
        HLT
A,      5
B,      7
hints: TAD A adds the value stored at label A. Do it twice: TAD A then TAD B.
successText: AC must equal 12 (that's 5 plus 7) at the halt.
reference:
*200
 CLA
 TAD A
 TAD B
 HLT
A, 5
B, 7
